ops.title: User steer (2026-07-19) — evaluate the score/effort function as hard as the model being scored; never treat a metric delta as signal without knowing the instrument's noise floor

ops.body:
While approving campaign experiment 2 the user said: *"stop assuming the score is everything, you should be evaluating the score/effort fn as much as you are the model being scored."*

**Why:** The P6 campaign's keep/discard treated sentinel-AUC 0.9317 < 0.9326 (Δ0.0009 on a ~631-row frame) as a clean negative without asking whether the instrument can resolve 0.001 — nobody has measured the score function's seed/sampling variance. The project already paid for this class of blindness once: the Tinker-frame parity gate rejected a good candidate until eval-what-you-serve replaced the instrument. The metric also prices no effort/cost axis, so "optimize AUC per dollar" is being decided by a function nobody audits.

**Measured (2026-07-19):** identical recipe/commit/seed/spec-digest score-watcher baselines scored 0.9326 vs 0.9180 hours apart — a between-run band of 0.0146, ~13× the margins both campaign verdicts were decided on (−0.0009 discard, +0.0011 keep). Neither verdict was signal.

**How to apply:** In any evaluation loop (campaigns, promotion gates, sweeps, audits): (1) report metric deltas against an estimated noise floor of the instrument, not as raw signal; (2) treat proposals/changes that probe the instrument itself (selector, val carve, frame, effort weighting) as first-class experiments, not scope creep; (3) when a verdict hinges on a small delta, spend on measuring instrument variance before spending on more candidates. Related: [[cc-steer-v0.10-verdict-identity-canonical-key]], [[dont-invent-unstated-constraints]].
